## [Web Book of Regional Science](https://researchrepository.wvu.edu/rri-web-book)

![Regional Governance, Institutions and Development](https://researchrepository.wvu.edu/rri-web-book/1029/thumbnail.jpg)

#### Title

[Regional Governance, Institutions and Development](https://researchrepository.wvu.edu/cgi/viewcontent.cgi?article=1029&context=rri-web-book)

#### Authors

[**Michael Danson**](https://researchrepository.wvu.edu/do/search/?q=author%3A%22Michael%20Danson%22&start=0&context=11616016)  
[**Geoff Whittam**](https://researchrepository.wvu.edu/do/search/?q=author%3A%22Geoff%20Whittam%22&start=0&context=11616016)

#### Files

Download Full Text (2.5 MB)

#### Description

Across the world, the regional level is becoming increasingly important in economic development with a proliferation of regionally based initiatives. This has important consequences for how institutional capacity is able to take and influence decisions with regard to the long-term future development of particular localities: in short the patterns of regional governance. This book focuses on two relevant aspects: (i) the question of governance - how does the ongoing process of institution-building affect the ways in which the regions and localities are governed, including questions of democracy, participation, regional self-determination, public-private partnerships and accountability; and (ii) what are the consequences of new modes of governance and institutional change for regional development strategies and policies, particularly in the context of large-scale industrial restructuring and city-region and urban regeneration.

#### Publication Date

2020

#### Publisher

Regional Research Institute, West Virginia University

#### Original Publication Information

#### City

Morgantown

#### Keywords

regionally based initiatives, governance, institutional change

The Recommendation lays out ten complementary pillars of regional development policy related to: 1) strategy; 2) territorial scale; 3) co-production; 4) resilience; 5) data; 6) multi-level governance; 7) capacities; 8) resources; 9) integrity; and 10) performance. b) systematically using data and evidence from monitoring and evaluation to better understand and communicate policy performance and impacts over the short and long terms, assess the coherence of different policies in light of regional development goals, and inform new policy initiatives. **A well-designed performance management system enables policymakers from all levels of government to monitor, evaluate and thus, improve regional development policy outcomes**. Making performance data and reports publicly available also enables citizens and other stakeholders having access to information on the progress and effects of regional development policies in different places.

# Implementation Toolkit: OECD Recommendation on Regional Development Policy

The Implementation Toolkit supports the 2023 OECD Recommendation on Regional Development Policy, which is the first international standard aimed at strengthening regional policies. The toolkit offers practical guidance for government officials seeking to improve their regional development policies, by enabling a deeper understanding of their strengths and weaknesses and learning from OECD member country experiences and good practices.

Share

[Facebook](https://www.facebook.com/sharer.php?u=https%3A%2F%2Fwww.oecd.org%2Fen%2Fabout%2Fprojects%2Fimplementing-the-oecd-recommendation-on-regional-development-policy-toolkit.html)
[Twitter](https://twitter.com/intent/tweet?url=https%3A%2F%2Fwww.oecd.org%2Fen%2Fabout%2Fprojects%2Fimplementing-the-oecd-recommendation-on-regional-development-policy-toolkit.html)
[LinkedIn](https://www.linkedin.com/sharing/share-offsite/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fwww.oecd.org%2Fen%2Fabout%2Fprojects%2Fimplementing-the-oecd-recommendation-on-regional-development-policy-toolkit.html)

## About

The OECD Recommendation on Regional Development Policy [[OECD/LEGAL/0492](https://legalinstruments.oecd.org/en/instruments/OECD-LEGAL-0492)], adopted by the OECD Council at Ministerial level in June 2023, provides an evidence-based and holistic framework to support OECD Members and non-Members having adhered to it, in designing and implementing effective regional development policies.

The Recommendation lays out ten complementary pillars of regional development policy related to: 1) strategy; 2) territorial scale; 3) co-production; 4) resilience; 5) data; 6) multi-level governance; 7) capacities; 8) resources; 9) integrity; and 10) performance. The full text of the Recommendation is available at Annex A.

[Recommendation of the Council on Regional Development Policy](https://legalinstruments.oecd.org/en/instruments/OECD-LEGAL-0492)

[Implementation Toolkit: OECD Recommendation on Regional Development Policy (PDF, 15 MB)](/content/dam/oecd/en/about/projects/cfe/implementing-the-oecd-recommendation-on-regional-development-policy-toolkit/toolkit-implementing-recommendation-on-regional-development-policy.pdf/_jcr_content/renditions/original./toolkit-implementing-recommendation-on-regional-development-policy.pdf)

## OECD Recommendation on Regional Development Policy

Implementation Toolkit

[Download the complete toolkit (PDF, 15 MB)](/content/dam/oecd/en/about/projects/cfe/implementing-the-oecd-recommendation-on-regional-development-policy-toolkit/toolkit-implementing-recommendation-on-regional-development-policy.pdf/_jcr_content/renditions/original./toolkit-implementing-recommendation-on-regional-development-policy.pdf)

## An Implementation Toolkit to turn the Recommendation into practice

To have impact, the Recommendation needs to be transformed into actions. The Recommendation includes a provision instructing the RDPC to support Adherents implement it through the development of an Implementation Toolkit that provides actionable measures and documents the range of initiatives and practices in place by Adherents, offering practical examples on how to effectively implement the provisions of the Recommendation.

This Toolkit provides concrete guidance to countries. It proposes actionable steps for Adherents to implement the ten pillars of the Recommendation while leveraging good practices from Adherents, and providing an important resource to non-Adherents, interested stakeholders and the public at large. It is designed primarily for national governments but is also of relevance to subnational governments. It should therefore be seen as a common framework of reference for policy makers, decision makers and relevant stakeholders at all levels of government to further exchange, build capacity, and thereby accelerate, when appropriate, the adoption of policies, governance and practices that contribute to regional development policy and sustainable development, including through engagement with a wide array of stakeholders (e.g. the private sector, academics, NGOs, citizens).

The Toolkit is considered a “live” resource, to be developed incrementally over time and enriched by new developments in Adherent countries. It builds on established OECD resources and tools, and will incorporate new ones, as further knowledge and operational guidance in any of the areas set out by the Recommendation become available. Furthermore, the Toolkit can support the RDPC in reporting to the OECD Council on the implementation, dissemination and continued relevance of the Recommendation, which is expected no later than five years following its adoption and at least every ten years thereafter.

## Pillar 1: Strategy

### 

**1. Design and implement an integrated and balanced regional development strategy tailored to different places**, by:

a) defining together with subnational governments and communities, clear and differentiated objectives for regional development that ultimately foster the contribution of all regions to national economic performance, well-being, environmental sustainability, resilience, and the reduction of regional inequalities;

b) integrating a territorial lens across a complementary set of sectoral policies, investments, and public services to ensure they are mutually reinforcing and that the objectives of sectoral policies and regional development policy are aligned;

c) leveraging each region’s specific competitive and absolute advantages at the national and international levels, as well as endogenous drivers of productivity, well-being, and environmental sustainability;

d) putting people’s well-being at the centre of regional development policy by targeting and addressing the specific needs of all populations, including vulnerable, under-represented, or marginalised groups;

e) supporting regions to develop sound approaches to enhance regional attractiveness to international investors, talent, visitors, and promote their integration in global markets and value chains.

### 

**By recognising geographic diversity and regional differences, and addressing them specifically, governments can create more effective and equitable policies.** Every region has unique strengths, challenges, and needs. A regional development strategy that considers territorial diversity, and tailor policies to different territorial contexts, helps prevent some places from being left behind while others advance, promoting overall national prosperity. When regions are relatively balanced in their socio-economic development, they contribute more evenly to the country's economy, reducing disparities and fostering a sense of unity and cohesion among the population. Designing and implementing an integrated and balanced regional development strategy tailored to different places is therefore crucial for governments to ensure that all places within a country can grow and thrive.

**A tailored, place-based regional development strategy can improve the quality of life for people in all places.** By focusing on the specific needs of each region – whether it is improving infrastructure in rural areas, supporting high-tech industries in urban centres, or promoting tourism in scenic places – governments can ensure that resources are used efficiently, and benefits are felt broadly. This can lead to better job opportunities, improved public services, and enhanced living conditions across the board. When people see tangible improvements in their communities, it can build trust in government and motivates further civic engagement, creating a positive cycle of growth and development.

### 

* Engage with a broad range of stakeholders – including national and subnational public decision-makers, businesses, civil society, academics and citizens – early in the process to identify development trends, regional challenges and priorities for regional development.
* Set long-term, people-centric objectives for regional development that reflect the specific priorities and assets of individual regions, while promoting balanced development across the country.
* Ensure coherence between national objectives and subnational strategies, so regional and local development plans align with and contribute to, overarching national goals.
* Review and adapt sectoral policies to account for differentiated regional impacts, and align them with regional development goals, fostering complementarities across sectors and territories.
* Design sectoral policies with territorial diversity in mind, considering the unique contexts, assets and challenges of each region.
* Leverage regions’ endogenous potential and comparative advantages – such as natural assets, markets, infrastructure, skills, and innovation capacities, including in emerging sectors – to drive sustainable growth.
* Incorporate multi-dimensional well-being metrics into regional development strategies to assess and promote societal progress beyond GDP.
* Assess and enhance regions’ attractiveness in a global context, identifying international linkages and key policy levers to attract investment, talent and visitors.
* Include internationalisation objectives within the regional development strategies to strengthen global engagement and competitiveness.

### 

* Setting national objectives for regional development in a top-down manner, without considering insights from regional and local stakeholders.
* Developing strategies with no specified time frame or that are too short (e.g. less 10 years) in their periodicity.
* Oversimplifying the regional development strategy to simple list of short- and medium-term investment projects.
* Designing a regional development strategy that is uniform and does not account for the diversity of territorial contexts.
* Defining regional development objectives focused only on economic performance and not on societal progress and people’s quality of life.
* Failing to articulate the regional development strategy with national sectoral policies and with other regional strategies that have a differentiated impact across regions.
* Failing to consider/identify which public and non-governmental actors will be responsible for implementing the strategy and whether they have necessary resources (e.g. financial, human, information) and policy tools (e.g. planning responsibilities) to do so.
* Failing to clearly identify and mandate a public body or co-ordination mechanism responsible for guiding and supervising strategy implementation.

### 

### Australia

In Australia, the Department of Infrastructure, Transport, Regional Development, Communications, and the Arts (DITRDCA) advises Australian Government departments and agencies on regional impact considerations for new policy proposals. It provides advice on completing Regional Australia Impact Statements (RAIS), as part of the policy development and Cabinet Submission process to help policy makers assess how new policy proposals affect regional, remote, and rural areas compared to metropolitan regions of Australia. The RAIS also supports consideration for how new policy proposals align with the Government's Regional Investment Framework (RIF), which sets out a coordinated approach to regional investment. The RIF prioritises informed, evidence-based decision-making and values local voices and priorities in shaping policy. It focuses on smart and responsible investments that help regions adapt and thrive, regardless of economic conditions. By promoting collaboration across governments, the RIF aims to improve outcomes for regional communities, industries, and economies. Ultimately, it ensures investment is transparent, targeted, and beneficial to regional Australia's long-term prosperity .

### Canada

Canada's Rural Economic Development Strategy, established in 2024, provides a place-based approach to support business development, connectivity, and regional partnerships. Key initiatives include extending high-speed internet and wireless connectivity nationwide, strengthening rural economies for job creation, renewing infrastructure to boost growth, and protecting rural communities from climate change. The Strategy aims to prepare rural Canadians for current and future jobs, ensure affordable housing, attract newcomers to rural areas, and foster collaboration through partnerships. The Strategy offers a roadmap and identifies complementary strategies from the Federal government.

### Germany

In Germany, the State of Brandenburg implemented the Connecting Strengths strategy based on the promotion of core regional growth areas and clusters. The strategy capitalises on regional “strengths” including new forms of work and technologies, renewable energy, mobility, organic farming and tourism, while leveraging vertical and horizontal co-ordination among actors across themes, sectors and ministries. In the future, the strategy will evolve with an approach based on growth corridors. The aim is to better connect metropolitan and rural areas by strategically connecting people, businesses, governments and R&D along existing railway lines .

### Italy

Italy introduced the National Strategy for Inner Areas (SNAI) in 2013 to revitalise sparsely populated regions struggling with depopulation and limited access to essential services. These "Inner Areas" cover nearly 60% of the country’s territory, encompassing than 1000 municipalities and home to 2 million inhabitants, and are characterised by their distance from major service centres. SNAI is a place-based policy built on a new multi-level governance approach, aiming to tackle demographic decline by leveraging local assets, enhancing infrastructure, and ensuring access to essential services such as healthcare, education, and transport. The strategy is backed by a budget of over €591 million, supplemented by resources from EU Operational Programmes, to implement concrete actions that promote sustainable territorial competitiveness, counter population decline, and drive economic growth  (Agenzia per la Coesione Territoriale, 2021[4]).

### Mexico

Mexico’s federal planning law requires development programmes at various government levels to align with the six-year National Development Plan, which sets long-term national priorities across economic, social, cultural, and environmental sectors. These programmes include institutional plans for public bodies, sectoral plans for specific industries, special programmes for cross-sectoral issues, and regional plans for geographic areas beyond state boundaries. In 2019, the Ministry of Finance and Public Funds held workshops to help national planning staff integrate cross-cutting priorities such as sustainability, equality, and gender into these programmes. Additionally, a government website was created to provide policymakers with resources, including factsheets on key economic, social, and environmental indicators, to inform programme diagnostics. This approach ensures a coordinated, data-driven strategy for addressing national and regional development challenges while promoting inclusivity and sustainable growth .

## Pillar 2: Territorial scale(s)

### 

**2. Target the appropriate territorial scale(s) for policy action to account for all types of interdependencies across and within regions, including through win-win partnerships**, by:

a) taking into account functional areas, as a complement to established administrative boundaries of subnational governments, that reflect the potential economic, social and environmental connections across territories;

integrating urban and rural areas, including through joint strategies, to maximise their synergies, enhance the production of public goods, achieve economies of scale in public service provision, and develop new economic and social opportunities.

### 

**Different regions and places are connected through economic, social, and environmental networks**. By addressing these interdependencies at the relevant territorial scale – whether local, regional, or national – policymakers can ensure that actions in one area positively influence others. This holistic approach helps create policies that are more coherent and efficient, avoiding unintended negative consequences that might arise from a narrower focus. For instance, infrastructure improvements in a metropolitan area can benefit surrounding rural regions through better connectivity and economic opportunities. Policy actions at the functional scale can complement actions taken according to administrative boundaries.

**Targeting the right territorial scale facilitates the creation of win-win partnerships between regions.** There is a spatial continuity and functional relations between urban and rural areas. The places where people live, work and consume span across these areas. When governments recognise and leverage the strengths of different places, they can foster collaborations that lead to mutual benefits. For example, a coastal city with a thriving port can partner with inland agricultural regions to enhance trade and economic growth. Such partnerships not only boost the economies of the involved regions but also contribute to national development. By promoting collaboration and shared goals, governments can build more resilient and sustainable regional networks, ensuring that all regions can support each other and grow together. This strategic approach helps in creating a balanced and inclusive development framework, where no region is left behind.

### 

* Define functional areas across the entire national territory, using internationally standardised definitions (e.g. OECD) to ensure consistency.
* Develop statistical indicators at the functional area level to complement existing data at the administrative level (regions, provinces, municipalities).
* Leverage functional area data and evidence to design spatially targeted policies and improve policy relevance.
* Engage subnational governments to align efforts and foster cross-jurisdictional and facilitate collaboration within and across functional areas.
* Foster urban-rural integration by promoting a shared understanding of socio-economic conditions and including urban-rural linkages in regional development policies and programmes.
* Support integrated territorial development strategies and inter-municipal partnerships, including for shared service delivery at the functional scale.
* Adopt enabling legislation and policy tools – such as contracts and financial incentives – to formalise and promote urban-rural and interjurisdictional partnerships.

### 

* Defining regional development policy measures based only on administrative boundaries.
* Setting objectives for rural and urban areas in silos, without considering their interdependencies.
* Failing to provide incentives to plan and deliver development strategies at the functional scale, including joint economic development projects or public service provision.

### 

### European Union

In 2015, the Integrated Territorial Investment (ITI) was introduced in the Common Provision Regulation as a tool for the European Structural and Investment Funds programming period. It allows EU countries to implement Operational Programmes in a cross-cutting way and to draw on funding from several priority axes of one or more Operational Programmes to ensure the implementation of an integrated strategy for a specific territory. As such, the existence of ITI both provides flexibility for EU countries regarding the design of Operational Programmes and enables the efficient implementation of integrated actions through simplified financing. The European Commission provides support to regions willing to set up ITIs in the form of networking and capacity-building events in particular through the Urban Development Network. The key elements of an ITI are i) a designated territory and an integrated territorial development strategy; ii) a package of actions to be implemented; and iii) and governance arrangements to manage the ITI .

### Israel

In recent years, voluntary associations of neighboring local governments, known as "clusters," have been established in Israel. Initially formed through a bottom-up approach, such as the Western Galilee Cluster in 2009, a formal pilot program was introduced in 2012 by the Ministry of Interior, the Ministry of Finance, and civil society organisations. Legislation later granted clusters legal status, and by 2019, 11 regional clusters had been created, primarily in Northern and Southern Israel, involving 120 local governments and 12% of the population. Clusters operate differently depending on their structure. Some rely on voluntary contributions from member governments, requiring agreement both to join the cluster and to participate in specific projects. The Western Galilee Cluster follows an alternative model, overseeing services such as environmental protection, waste removal, veterinary services, and specialised education programs across 18 small local councils, serving 200,000 residents in 115 communities . By 2024, the clusters included 175 local authorities, representing 67.5% of all local governments and 3.6 million residents, 37.4% of all residents of the country.

### Spain

Spain’s *Recovery, Transformation and Resilience* national strategy includes strengthening urban-rural linkages as one of its five main objectives. It sees those linkages as key to improving territorial cohesion and includes several relevant actions. The overall objective is to take advantage of nearby agglomeration economies to help attract venture capital as a necessary element to create and grow businesses in both urban and rural areas

### Türkiye

Türkiye’s Sustainable and Integrated Development Strategy recognises that the lines between rural and urban areas are increasingly blurred due to the effects of globalisation. The demography, labour market, goods and services markets, public services, and environmental externalities of rural and urban areas reveal mutual dependencies. The strategy considers those linkages from the perspective of geography or functional region, independent of administrative boundaries. It envisages their development that starts by recognising the interdependence of rural and urban areas and moves towards partnerships and, ultimately, rural-urban integration. Along these lines, Türkiye’s Urban and Rural Settlement Systems Research Project revealed an important finding when the structure of integrated rural service centres is evaluated together with the structure of integrated urban service centres. In areas where there is a high degree of agglomeration of urban settlements, there are also numerous rural settlements. In other words, strong urban systems are usually supported by strong rural systems, and vice-versa. In terms of settlements, this indicates that rural and urban areas are not rivals or alternatives but rather as complement. The project identified five main categories of interactions: demography (commuting); economy (labour, goods and service markets); delivery of public services; access to natural resources and environmental externalities (climate change, landscape, etc.); and governance (multi-level)

## Pillar 3: Co-production

### 

**3. Engage actively with regional and local communities and stakeholders throughout the policy-making cycle to gather and co-produce the knowledge needed to identify regions’ needs and leverage their specific assets**, by:

a) creating an enabling environment for participatory and deliberative processes involving all stakeholders and regional and local communities, including citizens, civil society, the private sector, labour groups, financial and educational institutions, and social partners, with adequate capacity and resources;

b) promoting strategic communication channels, transparency and access to clear, complete, timely, reliable, and relevant information, data, and evidence.

### 

**By involving those who live and work in regions, governments can gather valuable on-the-ground knowledge that might otherwise be overlooked**. Local stakeholders, including businesses, community leaders, civil society organisations, and residents as well as marginalised and underrepresented groups – have firsthand experience and insights into the challenges and opportunities in their regions. A collaborative approach leverages the insights of regional and local stakeholders to better understand the specific needs of their respective areas. Citizen participation and stakeholder engagement are useful tools for governments at all levels to gather valuable inputs for decision-making.

**Co-producing knowledge with regional and local stakeholders fosters a sense of ownership and empowerment within the community**. Broad and collaborative stakeholder engagement can increase the acceptability of policies by local actors and can contribute to the success and sustainability of policies. This participatory process builds trust between the government and the community, although the design of the engagement process determines its legitimacy and impact. The participatory and engagement process should clearly identify the policy issue to be addressed and set clear expectations regarding the intended outcome of the process. It is equally important to identify the societal group to involve and select a suitable methodology for participation and engagement. These definitions should also consider the phase of the policy cycle that the participatory process will address. For example, citizen deliberation can be a useful tool to identify policy priorities, but a meaningful exercise will require these processes to take place early in the planning phase rather than during implementation when the initiatives have already been designed.

**Engaging with regional and local communities also helps to identify and nurture unique regional strengths and assets that can be leveraged for broader development goals**. Each region has distinct cultural, economic, and environmental characteristics that can be harnessed to promote growth and resilience. For instance, a region known for its rich cultural heritage can develop cultural-based or tourism-based economic strategies, while an area with strong agricultural potential can focus on advancing sustainable farming practices. By working closely with local stakeholders, governments can ensure that these unique assets are recognised, preserved, and effectively integrated into development plans. This targeted approach can not only boost regional economies but can also contributes to the overall diversity and vibrancy of the national landscape.

### 

* Map relevant stakeholders at all levels of government who are involved in or affected by regional development policy.
* Develop a comprehensive stakeholder engagement strategy with clear objectives, aligned to the lifecycle and purpose of regional development policies.
* Define the purpose, target groups and expectations of the engagement process to ensure relevance and clarity.
* Select appropriate engagement methodologies tailored to the objectives and target audiences.
* Ensure engagement processes are inclusive, transparent and balanced, preventing capture by special interest groups and representing diverse perspectives.
* Build participation capacity among both policy makers and citizens – training officials to design and manage participatory processes, and supporting citizens to engage meaningfully.
* Promote civic education to equip citizens with the knowledge, skills and confidence to take part in public-decision making.
* Facilitate inclusive participation by reaching out to communities, especially those affected by regional policies, and making the process accessible, understandable and welcoming.
* Establish effective communication channels, including digital tools, to ensure timely, accessible information and engagement opportunities for all communities.

### 

* Designing and implementing regional development policy without considering the insights and knowledge of stakeholders and communities in regions.
* Engaging stakeholders in regional development policymaking as a “tick-the-box” exercise but without making meaningful use of their inputs.
* Participatory exercises with unbalanced representation of stakeholders, privileging one group over a

URL: https://media.licdn.com/dms/image/D5612AQERL2y4Y3d_Aw/article-cover_image-shrink_720_1280/0/1716343873777?e=2147483647&v=beta&t=siuO03IrUGwSOfAWrZIh8II1RERd_e2Z6NH8zU-ocPs

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

enriched_points.expanded_content: Effective governance structures ensure accountability, transparency, and participation, critical for implementing regional policies that adapt to local needs and foster sustainable development.

Sufficient financial resources are essential for executing regional development strategies, enabling investment in infrastructure, services, and innovation to stimulate economic growth and community resilience.

Engaging local communities in decision-making processes empowers citizens, builds trust, and harnesses unique regional knowledge, which is vital for tailoring strategies to specific regional contexts.
Implementing flexible, adaptive strategies allows for responsiveness to changing conditions and challenges, ensuring that regional development initiatives remain relevant and effective over time.
